elements.sent-2411-003: Real-world applications of stereo matching, such as autonomous driving, place stringent demands on both safety and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, learning-based stereo matching methods inherently suffer from the loss of geometric structures in certain feature channels, creating a bottleneck in achieving precise detail matching.
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, these methods lack interpretability due to the black-box nature of deep learning.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this paper, we propose MoCha-V2, a novel learning-based paradigm for stereo matching.
elements.sent-2411-007: MoCha-V2 introduces the Motif Correlation Graph (MCG) to capture recurring textures, which are referred to as ``motifs" within feature channels.
elements.sent-2411-008: These motifs reconstruct geometric structures and are learned in a more interpretable way.
elements.sent-2411-009: Subsequently, we integrate features from multiple frequency domains through wavelet inverse transformation.
elements.sent-2411-010: The resulting motif features are utilized to restore geometric structures in the stereo matching process.
elements.sent-2411-011: Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of MoCha-V2.
elements.sent-2411-012: MoCha-V2 achieved 1st place on the Middlebury benchmark at the time of its release.
elements.sent-2411-014: Creating a pixel-level disparity map is the main goal of binocular stereo matching \cite{lecunstereo .}
elements.sent-2411-015: This map can be used to calculate the depth of pixels in an image.
elements.sent-2411-016: The edge performance of the disparity map is particularly important for pixel-level rendering technologies such as virtual reality and augmented reality, where the exact match between scene model and image mapping is critical.
elements.sent-2411-017: This emphasises the importance of keeping the edges of the disparity map and the original RGB image precisely aligned.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{img/motivation.jpg} \caption{In the deep learning process, feature channels are extended to higher dimensions. During this process, the geometric details learned in individual feature channels are inevitably lost. We propose that these details can be recovered by identifying recurrent geometric structures across the channels. MoCha-V2 constructs a directed graph, i.e., motif correlation graph (MCG), to capture frequent patterns in feature channels. Specifically, MCG leverages node weights to detect recurring geometric structures within the channels. MCG establishes a robust and stable framework for identifying repetitive geometric structures. Due to its interpretability, MoCha-V2 also demonstrates significant potential for safety-critical real-world applications. } \label{motivation}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.8 \linewidth]{img/MoCha1.jpg} \caption{Pipeline of our previous work, MoCha-Stereo \cite{mocha}. MoCha-Stereo first constructs the Motif Channel Correlation Volume (MCCV). MCCV is build by projecting the relationship between motif channels and normal channels into the basic group correlation volume. Subsequently, we employ a iterative update operator to caculate a disparity, accroding to the correlation value. Finally, the Reconstruction Error Motif Penalty (REMP) module is applied to penalize the generation of the full-resolution disparity map. In REMP, $LFE$ refers to the Low-frequency Error branch, $LMC$ denotes to the Latent Motif Channel branch, and $HFE$ means the High-frequency Error branch. } \label{v1pipeline}

elements.sent-2411-020: With the development of deep learning \cite{simonyan2014very , learning-based methods have exhibited great potential in this field.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Stereo convolutional neural networks (CNNs) \cite{lecun1998gradient typically consist of five steps: feature extraction, cost volume construction, cost aggregation, disparity computation, and disparity refinement \cite{survey .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Cost volume gives preliminary similarity estimates for left image pixels and perhaps comparable right image pixels.
elements.sent-2411-023: The accuracy and computational complexity of the final result depend heavily on a clear and informative cost volume representation from this phase.
elements.sent-2411-024: A number of studies have been carried out to develop more accurate matching costs.
elements.sent-2411-025: A common approach is to construct a 4D cost volume \cite{lecunstereo,gcnet,psmnet .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Specifically, 4D cost volume connects the left feature map with its corresponding right feature map and records the disparity at this point to form a cost volume.
elements.sent-2411-027: This process constructs a space with dimensions of $height \times width \times disparity \times feature$ $values$, which serves as the cost volume.
elements.sent-2411-028: To reduce computational costs, RAFT-Stereo \cite{raftstereo designed a 3D correlation pyramid \cite{raft .}}

elements.sent-2411-030: CGEV encodes the geometric relationship between the left and right views.

elements.sent-2411-036: Although existing methods attempting to optimize the network from multiple steps, SOTA stereo matching methods still struggle to accurately match details.
elements.sent-2411-037: As illustrated in Figure \ref{motivation , we believe this occurs because CNNs are essentially black-box nonlinear transformations.}
elements.sent-2411-038: CNNs map images from low-dimensional to high-dimensional spaces, and this process cannot guarantee appropriate activation values for each channel.
elements.sent-2411-039: The unbalanced activation inevitably leads to the loss of edge texture information.

elements.sent-2411-043: In time series analysis, recurring segments of time sequences are called ``motifs".
elements.sent-2411-044: Similarly, recurrent geometric structures exist within the feature channels of images.
elements.sent-2411-045: We refer to the feature channel that encapsulates this repeated geometric information as the ``motif channel".
elements.sent-2411-046: These geometric structures help to recover missing edge information in the feature channels.
elements.sent-2411-047: As shown in Figure \ref{v1pipeline , we employ a sliding window to capture the motif channels.}
elements.sent-2411-048: We then map the motif channel back to the original feature channel to fill in missing details.

elements.sent-2411-061: In summary, our main contributions are as follow:.
elements.sent-2411-062: We propose MoCha-Stereo and its improved version, MoCha-V2.
elements.sent-2411-063: In MoCha-V2, we introduce a white-box motif channel mining scheme, namely the Motif Correlation Graph, to extract recurrent geometric features and restore edge details in the feature channels.
elements.sent-2411-064: We further considered recurring low-frequency features as part of the motif channel.
elements.sent-2411-065: This enabled MoCha-V2 to achieve more accurate matching performance.
elements.sent-2411-066: At the time of submission, MoCha-V2 was ranked $1^{st$} on the Middlebury (Bad 1.0 all) benchmark, and $2^{nd$} on KITTI 2012 (Reflective) benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-067: Mining motifs can be utilized to identify recurring patterns, detect anomalies, and perform pattern matching.
elements.sent-2411-068: Motifs are particularly useful for tasks such as time series classification and clustering \cite{matrix1,matrix2,matrix5 .}
elements.sent-2411-069: The formal definition of a motif is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-070: In a time series $T$, there exists a subsequence $T_{i,L}$, which starts from the $i$-th position in the time series $T$ and is a continuous subset of values with a length of $L$.
elements.sent-2411-071: Motif is the pair of subsequences $T_{a,L}$ and $T_{b,L}$ in a time series that are the most similar.
elements.sent-2411-072: In mathematical notation, For case $\forall i,j \in [1,2,...,n-L+1]$ and $a \neq b\ , \ i \neq j$, the motif \cite{matrix2 satisfies as Equation \ref{motif . \begin{equation}\label{motif}  dist(T_{a,L},T_{b,L}) \leq dist(T_{i,L},T_{j,L}) \end{equation} where $dist$ means a distance measure.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The distances between all subsequences and their nearest neighbors are stored as Matrix Profile (MP) \cite{matrix1 , representing the repetitive pattern (motif) in the time series.}
elements.sent-2411-074: In stereo matching, the geometric structure also repeats across binocular views.
elements.sent-2411-075: However, the computation of motif patterns (MP) in time series is not directly applicable to stereo matching.
elements.sent-2411-076: Unlike time series, the repetitive patterns (or motifs) in images are two-dimensional.
elements.sent-2411-077: The MP computation in time series is computationally expensive for images, as it requires multiple samplings of subsequences.
elements.sent-2411-078: Additionally, selecting sub-patches from multi-channel image features to compute similarity incurs significant computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-079: To address this problem, we have developed a collection of sliding windows, which are arranged into two-dimensional vectors to capture recurring geometric patterns.
elements.sent-2411-080: These patterns are then stored as ``motif channels".
elements.sent-2411-081: The ability to match details is crucial for stereo matching.
elements.sent-2411-082: Consequently, numerous methods concentrate on edge mining.
elements.sent-2411-083: Accurate detail matching is critical for stereo matching, prompting many methods to focus on edge mining.
elements.sent-2411-084: SegStereo \cite{segstereo leverages a segmentation network to generate feature maps.}
elements.sent-2411-085: EdgeStereo \cite{edgestereo utilizes an edge detection network to aggregate edge features.}
elements.sent-2411-086: IGEV-Stereo \cite{igev encodes geometric features to improve local matching.}
elements.sent-2411-087: LoS \cite{los incorporates specialized branches to capture local structure.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The effectiveness of the state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods discussed above is evident.
elements.sent-2411-089: However, existing approaches fail to address some inherent limitations of convolutional neural networks (CNNs): 1) CNNs primarily perform non-linear transformations \cite{cam , and imbalanced activations can lead to detail loss across various feature channels \cite{visualizing .}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Attention mechanisms are widely utilized in neural networks and can be categorized into three main types: spatial attention \cite{stn , channel attention \cite{cama , and mixed attention \cite{cbam .}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The Spatial Transformer Network \cite{stn exemplifies a spatial attention module (SAM), which employs convolution to generate a weight map for features in the spatial domain.}
elements.sent-2411-092: However, spatial attention does not account for channel-wise differences.
elements.sent-2411-093: The Channel Attention Module (CAM) \cite{cama addresses this limitation by assigning distinct weights to each feature channel.}
elements.sent-2411-094: FcaNet \cite{fcanet further enhances this by decoupling the global average pooling (GAP) process and leveraging frequency-domain information to better squeeze the channels.}
elements.sent-2411-095: While channel attention is beneficial for visual understanding, it tends to overlook finer edge details compared to spatial attention.
elements.sent-2411-096: To address these limitations, mixed attention \cite{cbam, wu2024distribution has been proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Mixed attention typically combines CAM and SAM either in series or in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-098: One of the foundational works in this area is the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) \cite{cbam .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Stereo matching networks also benefit from attention mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-100: DLNR \cite{DLNR utilizes channel attention \cite{cama to enhance feature representations in its Channel Transformer Blocks.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Selective-Stereo \cite{selective extends the CBAM framework by proposing the Contextual Spatial Attention Module.}
elements.sent-2411-102: However, several characteristics of attention modules make them unsuitable for direct application in stereo matching.
elements.sent-2411-103: First, both detailed and semantic information are crucial for accurate stereo matching.
elements.sent-2411-104: Current mixed attention methods \cite{cbam,selective typically treat these aspects separately.}
elements.sent-2411-105: While the objectives of spatial attention and channel attention differ: spatial attention primarily focuses on edge details, and channel attention is more concerned with semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-106: Learning these attention types in isolation leads to a compromise in capturing both geometric details and global semantics.
elements.sent-2411-107: Second, existing attention mechanisms do not consider the correlation between the left and right views, which is crucial for stereo matching tasks.
elements.sent-2411-108: Finally, edge information is often represented by high-frequency signals.
elements.sent-2411-109: It is more efficient to extract spatial features by incorporating frequency-domain signals, as this enables better capture of edge details.
elements.sent-2411-110: This paper proposes the Motif Correlation Graph Attention (MCGA) to address the aforementioned issues.
elements.sent-2411-111: Spatial and channel features are learned concurrently.
elements.sent-2411-112: The correlation between the left and right views is modeled as a correlation graph.
elements.sent-2411-113: Spatial features are extracted from the frequency domain.
elements.sent-2411-114: MoCha-Stereo \cite{mocha and MoCha-V2 provide solutions to mitigate the effects of imbalanced activations in CNNs.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Different from our conference version \cite{mocha , MoCha-V2 further enhances interpretability and accuracy.}

elements.sent-2411-264: MoCha-V2 demonstrates strong performance even with a reduced number of iterations.
elements.sent-2411-265: As shown in Table \ref{iter_abl , MoCha-V2 achieves SOTA results without the need for a large number of iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-266: With just 4 iterations, MoCha-V2 surpasses IGEV-Stereo \cite{igev (EPE: 0.47 px, time: 0.37 s) by over 4.3\% in accuracy and reduces inference time by a notable 45.9\%.}
elements.sent-2411-267: This allows users to balance time efficiency and performance according to their specific application needs.
elements.sent-2411-268: What is more, MoCha-V2 only needs to build one motif channel for each channel, allowing it to achieve faster inference times than MoCha-Stereo \cite{mocha .}

elements.sent-2411-270: This paper proposes MoCha-V2, a novel stereo matching network.
elements.sent-2411-271: Specifically, MoCha-V2 utilizes a white-box graph to focus on recurrent geometric details within the feature channel, enabling the preservation of edges during the deep learning process.
elements.sent-2411-272: This explainable detail-learning technique is crucial for the robustness of real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-273: Experiments validate the applicability of our approach to real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-274: Our MoCha-V2 achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on the Middlebury, KITTI, and ETH3D benchmarks and demonstrates strong generalization capability in unseen real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-275: While it is regrettable that MoCha-V2 is not a fully white-box stereo matching method, the deep learning processes outside of the Motif Correlation Graph (MCG) may still lead to safety concerns.
elements.sent-2411-276: The reason we have not yet achieved a fully white-box process is that the gap between interpretability techniques and the performance of deep learning modules is still significant, and a fully transparent approach currently struggles to achieve higher accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-277: We plan to further enhance the interpretability of learning-based stereo matching methods in the future.. \ifCLASSOPTIONcompsoc.
